**[Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism")** is the [largest religion in South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia#Religions "South Asia") with about 1.2 billion [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus"), forming just under two-thirds of [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia")'s population.[[a]](#cite_note-SouthAsiaPop-8)[[b]](#cite_note-HinduIndia-9)[[c]](#cite_note-HinduNepal-14)[[e]](#cite_note-HinduPakistan-21)[[f]](#cite_note-HinduSriLanka-25)[[g]](#cite_note-HinduBhutan-28)[[24]](#cite_note-pewforum.org-31) South Asia has the [largest population of Hindus in the world](/wiki/Hinduism_by_country "Hinduism by country"), with about 99% of all global [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") being from South Asia.[[25]](#cite_note-32) Hinduism is the dominant [religion in India](/wiki/Religion_in_India "Religion in India") and [Nepal](/wiki/Religion_in_Nepal "Religion in Nepal") and is the second-largest religion in [Bangladesh](/wiki/Religion_in_Bangladesh "Religion in Bangladesh"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Religion_in_Pakistan "Religion in Pakistan"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Religion_in_Sri_Lanka "Religion in Sri Lanka"), and [Bhutan](/wiki/Religion_in_Bhutan "Religion in Bhutan").[[26]](#cite_note-33)

[Indo-Aryan migrations](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_migrations "Indo-Aryan migrations") brought the [Indo-Aryans](/wiki/Indo-Aryans "Indo-Aryans") to [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"),[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlood199621–23-34) where they compiled and composed the Vedic corpus during the [Vedic period](/wiki/Vedic_period "Vedic period") (ca. 1500-500 BCE) across present-day [Northern India](/wiki/Northern_India "Northern India"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") and [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan").[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThapar196623-35) The subsequent period, between 800 BCE and 250 BCE, was "a turning point between the Vedic religion and Hindu religions", and a formative period for Hinduism, [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism") and [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"). The Epic and Early Puranic period, from c. 250 BCE and 500 CE, saw the onset of the [Hindu synthesis](/wiki/Hindu_synthesis "Hindu synthesis"), followed by the classical "Golden Age" of India (c. 350 - 650 CE), which coincides with the [Gupta Empire](/wiki/Gupta_Empire "Gupta Empire"). [[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHonourFleming2005123-141-36)

Following the [conquest](/wiki/Muslim_conquests_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Muslim conquests in the Indian subcontinent") of [Islamic rulers in the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Islamic_rulers_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Islamic rulers in the Indian subcontinent") and [spread of Islam in South Asia](/wiki/Islam_in_South_Asia "Islam in South Asia"), an era featuring [persecution of Hindus](/wiki/Persecution_of_Hindus "Persecution of Hindus") began and continued until the end of [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire").[[A]](#cite_note-38) The [Vijayanagara](/wiki/Vijayanagara_Empire "Vijayanagara Empire") and [Maratha Empire](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire") significantly protected and revived Hinduism in the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), while the [Jaffna Kingdom](/wiki/Jaffna_Kingdom "Jaffna Kingdom") and [Gorkha dynasty](/wiki/Gorkha_Kingdom "Gorkha Kingdom") have significantly protected [Hinduism in Sri Lanka](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Sri_Lanka "Hinduism in Sri Lanka") and [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") respectively.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHonourFleming2005123-141-36)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_South_Asia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Hinduism](/wiki/History_of_Hinduism "History of Hinduism")

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_South_Asia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins")]

The [Vedic period](/wiki/Vedic_period "Vedic period"), named after the Vedic religion of the [Indo-Aryans](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_peoples "Indo-Aryan peoples"), lasted from c. 1900 BCE-500 BCE. The Indo-Aryans were pastoralists who migrated into north-western India after the collapse of the Indus Valley Civilization. Linguistic and archaeological data show a cultural change in the subcontinent after 1500 BCE, with the linguistic and religious data clearly showing links with Indo-European languages and religion. By about 1500 BCE, the Vedic culture and agrarian lifestyle were established in the northwest and northern Gangetic plain of South Asia. Rudimentary state-forms appeared, of which the [Kuru](/wiki/Kuru_Kingdom "Kuru Kingdom")-Pañcāla union was the most influential. The first recorded [state-level society](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") in South Asia existed around 1000 BCE. In this period, states Samuel emerged the Brahmana and Aranyaka layers of Vedic texts, which merged into the earliest [Upanishads](/wiki/Upanishads "Upanishads"). These texts began to ask the meaning of a ritual, adding increasing levels of philosophical and metaphysical speculation, or "[Hindu synthesis](/wiki/Hindu_synthesis "Hindu synthesis")".

### Rise of Hindu Nationalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_South_Asia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Rise of Hindu Nationalism")]

Main article: [Hindu nationalism](/wiki/Hindu_nationalism "Hindu nationalism")

In recent years, there has been a rising movement of [Hindu nationalism](/wiki/Hindu_nationalism "Hindu nationalism") and feeling of [Hindutva](/wiki/Hindutva "Hindutva") or Hindu identity among the Hindus of India.[[31]](#cite_note-39) This has been observed especially after the formation of the [BJP](/wiki/BJP "BJP") government in India in 2014.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHindu_nationalism2019h-40) In India, several [Hindu nationalist political parties](/wiki/List_of_Hindu_nationalist_political_parties "List of Hindu nationalist political parties") exist, out of which the BJP is the biggest among them.[[33]](#cite_note-41) Alongside the BJP, the [Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh](/wiki/Rashtriya_Swayamsevak_Sangh "Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh") (RSS) has also been accused of being a chief organisation in the Hindu nationalist cause.[[34]](#cite_note-42)[[35]](#cite_note-43)

The rise of Hindu nationalism and Hindutva is seen as a threat to the [secular laws of India](/wiki/Secularism_in_India "Secularism in India").[[36]](#cite_note-44) It was also seen that the with rise of the Hindu nationalism, there has been an increase in persecutions of religious minorities, mainly [Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_India "Islam in India") and [Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_India "Christianity in India").[[37]](#cite_note-45)[[38]](#cite_note-46) The [government of Narendra Modi](/wiki/Premiership_of_Narendra_Modi "Premiership of Narendra Modi") has also been blamed for the increase in violence.[[39]](#cite_note-47) Other hardline Hindutva groups, such as the [Vishva Hindu Parishad](/wiki/Vishva_Hindu_Parishad "Vishva Hindu Parishad") (VHP)[[40]](#cite_note-48) and the [Bajrang Dal](/wiki/Bajrang_Dal "Bajrang Dal")[[41]](#cite_note-49) (which are designated as [paramilitary groups](/wiki/Paramilitary_groups "Paramilitary groups") by many nations such as [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"),[[42]](#cite_note-50) [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") and [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States")[[43]](#cite_note-51)) have also contributed a major role to the enhancement of Hindu nationalism and are also blamed for increasing [Islamophobia in India](/wiki/Islamophobia_in_India "Islamophobia in India") and [attacks on Christians](/wiki/Persecution_of_Christians_in_India "Persecution of Christians in India").[[44]](#cite_note-52)[[45]](#cite_note-53)

Similarly, Nepal has also experienced the same rise in Hindu nationalism, mainly after 2015 after demonstrators protested for the re-declaration of Hinduism as the [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") of Nepal along with the restoration of the [monarchy](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Nepal "Monarchy of Nepal") in the country. Nepal has seen an increase in violence against [Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_Nepal "Christianity in Nepal").[[46]](#cite_note-54)

## Temples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_South_Asia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Temples")]

Main article: [Hindu temple architecture](/wiki/Hindu_temple_architecture "Hindu temple architecture")

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_South_Asia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Demographics")]

**Percentage of Hindus by country**

|  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Country** | |  | **Percentage** | |
| [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") | |  | 81.34% | |
| [India](/wiki/India "India") | |  | 80% | |
| [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") | |  | 13.40% | |
| [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan") | |  | 22.50% | |

Hinduism is the majority religion in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), and most of the world's [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") are home to the region.[[47]](#cite_note-55) 5 of the [10 nations with the biggest Hindu populations](/wiki/Hinduism_by_country "Hinduism by country") are in South Asia, namely [India](/wiki/Hinduism_in_India "Hinduism in India"), [Nepal](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Nepal "Hinduism in Nepal"), [Bangladesh](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Bangladesh "Hinduism in Bangladesh"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Pakistan "Hinduism in Pakistan") and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Sri_Lanka "Hinduism in Sri Lanka"). [India](/wiki/India "India") is home to more than 1.2 billion Hindus, 94% of the world's global Hindu population.[[48]](#cite_note-56)

Hinduism is the majority religion in [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") and [India](/wiki/India "India"), with 81.34% and 79.8% of the countries' respective populations practicing Hinduism.[[B]](#cite_note-57) Hinduism is the second largest religious group in [Bangladesh](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Bangladesh "Hinduism in Bangladesh"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Pakistan "Hinduism in Pakistan"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Sri_Lanka "Hinduism in Sri Lanka") and [Bhutan](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Bhutan "Hinduism in Bhutan") and there is a very minute Hindu minority in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Afghanistan "Hinduism in Afghanistan").[[22]](#cite_note-Hindus_and_Sikhs_v6.0-29) There are also no Hindus in the [Maldives](/wiki/Hinduism_in_the_Maldives "Hinduism in the Maldives"), as per its constitution.[[C]](#cite_note-mAl8-59)

In recent years, Hindu populations have decreased in non-Hindu majority countries in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), especially [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") and [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). This is due to a number of reasons, such as [persecution](/wiki/Persecution_of_Hindus "Persecution of Hindus"), [forced conversion](/wiki/Forced_conversion "Forced conversion") and [low-fertility rates](/wiki/Fertility_rate "Fertility rate").[[50]](#cite_note-60)[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEaton199389-61) Civil war in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghan_conflict "Afghan conflict") and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_Civil_War "Sri Lankan Civil War") have also caused Hindus to flee these countries.[[52]](#cite_note-62)[[53]](#cite_note-63)
